issue_title: Database CLI still imports from config.DefaultConfig
Simulator issue when joystick axis is 1.0
issue_body:
Under hal_impl\function.py, line 237:
axes.axes = [_axes_list[int(a_128)+128] for a in hal_data['joysticks'][joystickNum]['axes']]
If a is equal to 1.0, _axes_list index will go out of range (int(1.0_128)+128) equals 256, but range of _axes_list only goes up to 255.

To fix, change both 128 scalar and offset to 127.5. 
axes.axes = [_axes_list[int(a*127.5+127.5)] for a in hal_data['joysticks'][joystickNum]['axes']]

issue_title: Support verification of GnuPG signed packages
issue_body:
You can upload packages to PyPI and sign the distributions using GnuPG with `twine` (https://packaging.python.org/tutorials/distributing-packages/#upload-your-distributions).

My recommendation for `pipenv` is that you can (optionally) add a GnuPG key to the piplock file for packages. The update or installation process will then only work if the packages has been signed with the correct GnuPG key.

While I agree that `pipenv` should not add too many features and focus on being relatively lighweight and functional, I think this is an important issue because of security. Manually veryfing signatures is not the way to go.

issue_title: Pip version in the Pipfile.lock
issue_body:
Bundler is an amazing tool in ruby-land.
I hope you guys study some of it, as it works really really well.
Specifically, A major pain point there was an addition of the specific "Bundler" version to the Gemfile.lock .

This was a painful migration, but later on allowed much more power to the Bundler authors, if it's in upgrades or in specific lock file changes.
I think if you add this and the specific bundler logic (only update when newer version exists) it will save much pain later on - 
https://github.com/bundler/bundler/issues/3697

issue_title: Lockfile doesn't include sub-dependencies
issue_body:
Originally [reported by](https://gist.github.com/kennethreitz/4745d35e57108f5b766b8f6ff396de85#gistcomment-1927092) @charettes:

> Shouldn't implicit dependencies be included in the lock files as well à la `pip freeze`?

Reasoning for that is simple: imagine package A depends on `B == 1.0`, and package B depends on `C >= 1.0`. Then C 1.1 comes out with some subtle changes that break B completely, and — BAM! — your build is broken.

The problem with this is, PyPI doesn't return packages dependencies (I've tried several APIs, no luck), so in order to lock dependencies we need t actually install them first. Doesn't seem like a big deal to me, though.

    version)
ValueError: patchelf  found. auditwheel repair requirespatchelf >= 0.9.
```

Interestingly, the auditwheel tests are not failing, despite the tracebacks thrown.

The problem is that `patchelf --version` doesn't output anything on version 0.9. I've heard @geofft has [submitted a patch to fix this upstream](https://github.com/rmcgibbo/pypatchelf/pull/1), and I've tested & confirmed the patch fixes the bug.

Hey folks, it seems that the fix that resolves the tornado channel issue isnt reflected when installing version 0.8 via PIP

pip install pusher==0.8
cat /usr/local/lib/python2.7/dist-packages/pusher/init.py

result:

```
class  TornadoChannel(Channel):
    def trigger(self, event, data={}, socket_id=None, callback=None):
        self.callback = callback
        return super(TornadoChannel, self).trigger(event, data, socket_id)

    def send_request(self, signed_path, data_string):
        import tornado.httpclient
        absolute_url = self.get_absolute_path(signed_path)
        request = tornado.httpclient.HTTPRequest(absolute_url, method='POST', body=data_string)
        client = tornado.httpclient.AsyncHTTPClient()
        client.fetch(request, callback=self.callback)
        # Returning 202 to avoid Channel errors. Actual error handling takes place in callback.
        return 202, ""
```

If its compared to what its actually in github, it differs

```
class TornadoChannel(Channel):
    def trigger(self, event, data={}, socket_id=None, callback=None, timeout=socket._GLOBAL_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT):
        self.callback = callback
        return super(TornadoChannel, self).trigger(event, data, socket_id, timeout=timeout)

    def send_request(self, signed_path, data_string, timeout=socket._GLOBAL_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT):
        timeout = None if timeout == socket._GLOBAL_DEFAULT_TIMEOUT else timeout
        import tornado.httpclient
        absolute_url = self.get_absolute_path(signed_path)
        request = tornado.httpclient.HTTPRequest(absolute_url, method='POST', body=data_string, request_timeout=timeout)
        client = tornado.httpclient.AsyncHTTPClient()
        client.fetch(request, callback=self.callback)
        # Returning 202 to avoid Channel errors. Actual error handling takes place in callback.
        return 202, ""
```

As i see, it just needs to be pushed to the PIP repos

issue_title: Cannot create producer on Python 3.5
issue_body:
I'm trying to create a simple producer in Python 3.5 and come across the following error message:
```
  File "/app/.tox/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pykafka/topic.py", line 88, in get_producer
    return Cls(self._cluster, self, **kwargs)
  File "/app/.tox/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 152, in __init__
    self.start()
  File "/app/.tox/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 189, in start
    self._setup_owned_brokers()
  File "/app/.tox/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 228, in _setup_owned_brokers
    self, partition.leader)
  File "/app/.tox/py35/lib/python3.5/site-packages/pykafka/producer.py", line 426, in __init__
    self.lock = self.producer._cluster.handler.RLock()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.5/threading.py", line 82, in RLock
    return _PyRLock(*args, **kwargs)
TypeError: __init__() got an unexpected keyword argument 'verbose'
```

I've tracked the problem down to the following line in `pykafka/handlers.py`:
```
    # turn off RLock's super annoying default logging
    RLock = functools.partial(threading.RLock, verbose=False)
```

So, what this seems to be doing is instantiating a new RLock, which is fine. But if we take a look at python 3.5's `threading.py` we see the following (I removed unnecessary info):

```
try:
    _CRLock = _thread.RLock
except AttributeError:
    _CRLock = None
 
   ...

def RLock(*args, **kwargs):
    if _CRLock is None:
        return _PyRLock(*args, **kwargs)
    return _CRLock(*args, **kwargs)

  ...

class _RLock:
    def __init__(self):
       ...

   ...

_PyRLock = _RLock
```

As you can see, if we don't have a CRLock, we end up using a PyRLock, which does not take any arguments. This causes the producer to fail being created since it gets an unexpected keyword argument.

issue_title: Consider using shiv instead of virtualenvs for deployment

issue_title: PSF homogenisation
issue_body:
I'm looking for some Python code to do PSF homogenisation.

I'm not aware of good references, a short description is here:
http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2011ASPC..442..435B

The use case we have in [Gammapy](https://github.com/gammapy/gammapy) is Fermi-LAT images in energy bands, and the Fermi-LAT PSF varies by almost two orders of magnitude from the lowest to the highest energies (see [here](http://www.slac.stanford.edu/exp/glast/groups/canda/lat_Performance_files/gPsfAve95Energy_P8R2_SOURCE_V6fb_10MeV.png)).
What I want is a function that gives me the kernel I need to use to smooth the data from multiple energy bands so that the smoothed images have the same shape for point sources.
In this case the PSF is radially symmetric, i.e. represented by 1-dim curves (in Gammapy we have the [TablePSF](https://gammapy.readthedocs.org/en/latest/api/gammapy.irf.TablePSF.html) class for that).

For example, let's assume we have two 2D Gaussian PSFs with width sigma1 and sigma2.
Then the image with sigma1 PSF can be smoothed with a 2D Gaussian of width sigma2, and the image with sigma2 PSF with a 2D Gaussian of width sigma1, and both smoothed images will have the same PSF of width `sqrt(sigma1 ** 2 + sigma2 ** 2)`. Gaussians are a special case where the homogenisation kernels can be computed analytically.

I'm not sure what a good, simple method is to do this for arbitrary PSFs.
This looks very sophisticated:
http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2011ASPC..442..435B
I'm really looking for something simpler / doesn't need to be super-precise.
E.g. one could assume some parametrised shape for the homogenisation kernels (like Gaussian or King profile) and fit the parameters e.g. using chi^2 as measure for the difference of homogenised PSFs.

Any ideas how to do this?
Is this appropriate for photutils or should we start implementing something in Gammapy?

expand = ['application', 'application_fee', 'balance_transaction',

issue_title: Optimization and functionality change for _initPermanence
issue_body:
In testing the NLP code I noticed that calls to `getReal64()` during initialization took 21s out of the total of 540s of execution time for the whole program. This is unnecessarily slow and can be optimized. 

1. `spatial_pooler.py` uses a python loop with individual scalar calls to `getReal64()` [here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1174), [here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1140), and [here](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1126). Preliminary testing shows that replacing this code with a vectorized call to `initializeReal32Array()` should produce a 15x speedup. Vectorizing the remainder of [this loop](https://github.com/numenta/nupic/blob/master/src/nupic/research/spatial_pooler.py#L1170-L1177) with numpy should lead to additional speedups. This can be implemented with no functional changes to the `_initPermanence` method, but may require removing `_initPermConnected` and `_initPermNonConnected` as separate methods.

2. A second issue brought up by @scottpurdy is that `connectedPct` only produces the correct number of connected synapses on average. In order to have an exact and repeatable number of connected synapses, a better approach would be to initialize an array with the desired number of 1s with 0s in the remaining places, and then shuffle the array. This would be a functional change that would require updating tests. Additionally, the nupic.core code would have to be updated for the functionality to match.

Point 1 can be implemented independently of point 2, but point 2 is only possible with the vectorization proposed in point 1.

@subutai

issue_title: records: expose past revisions
issue_body:
It would be useful to expose past revisions of records over the web.

Upon update of records, the past versions are all stored in the record
history table (`hstRECORD`).  The `bibedit` CLI offers functions to
inspect past versions, for example:

```
$ sudo -u www-data /opt/invenio/bin/bibedit --list-revisions 10
10.20140709180731
10.20140709180059
```

The record revision ID is of the form `RECID.YYYYMMHHhhmmss` where
`YYYY...ss` corresponds to the timestamp when the record was updated.
(Note that this equals to MARC tag `005`.)

Currently, the past record versions are exposed via the
above-mentioned `bibedit` CLI to the administrators, or in the record
editor UI panel to the cataloguers.  They are not easily exposed to
the regular end users.  It would be useful to expose past record
versions also via regular web interface, for example:

```
/record/18/export/xm?revision=REVID
/record/18?of=xm&revision=REVID
```

Task 1: implement the above `revision` argument to expose past master
formats in various record-accessing URLs and APIs, notably
`/record/NNN?of=XXX`.  This will provide 100% complete support for
exposing past historical versions of master formats.

There are several challenges here beyond implementing basic
functionality, notably regarding master-vs-derived formats and
regarding non-MARC master formats.

For example, let us say that BibTeX is an important output format for
a given Invenio installation.  Exposing past record versions in BibTeX
can be done in basically two ways: (a) take an old MARCXML master
format version and run it through the "latest greatest" BibTeX
conversion procedure using current conversion rules, as for the
current MARCXML master format; (b) take old MARCXML version and old
conversion rules, IOW take the old BibTeX format as it had been in
that past moment, and expose precisely that.  The former method has an
advantage that the output would be always "up-to-date" with current
BibTeX rules of the given installation, even in case the
MARCXML->BibTeX processing rules change, say when a new field is
added.  However, with the latter method the output would be "truer" to
what it has been at the given point in time of that old revision.

To use an analogy, one could say that:

```
incoming master format + transformation templates -> outgoing output format
```

where:

```
  marcxml + xslt -> dublincore
  marcxml + bibformat elements -> bibtex
  [...]
```

The former approach would call for using historical MARCXML version
with latest XSLT templates or bibformat elements, while the latter
approach would call for using even historical versions of templates
and bibformat elements.  (Which is not practical to do, since these
could use KB lookups or SQL lookups that are not preserved; hence it
would be better to cache the result of the conversion as a whole.)

The former can be considered a "best effort" support for past slave
formats (taking historical master format with latest conversion
templates).  The latter can be considered a "complete support" for
past history (taking pre-stored past slave formats).  To do this for
all slave formats can be quite time and space consuming, e.g. for a
site of 1M records, if every record contains 10 revisions and if we
store 1 master and 5 slave formats, then we'd have to store 1M*10*6
which is 60M records in our historical record store.

Ideally, we could introduce a configuration variable like
`CFG_BIBFORMAT_CACHED_FORMATS` where each site would define which
formats are considered "important" for an installation to keep the
historical versions of.  These formats would then be stored in the
historical record store alongside the master format, say during
`bibreformat` step.  This would give a chance to each site to decide
when to go for "best effort" support and when to go for "complete
support".

Task 2: extend history table `hstRECORD` (or introduce new store) that
would store any number of wanted formats of the record, depending on
the configuration.  (I.e. not only `marcxml` formats after record
updates.)  Extend all APIs to take advantage of such cached formats.

The same challenge applies to non-MARC master formats.  Their past
versions have to be stored in `hstRECORD`, so the table extension may
contain not `marcxml` column, but two `format_type` and `format_value`
columns.

Task 3: after we have some UNICODE demo records, write test cases for
processing non-MARC records in `hstRECORD` table.

Note that this is also very closely linked with versioning internal
JSON representation of records (`recjson`).  Currently, only latest
`recjson` representation of a record is stored in the record cache.
We may want to store past versions of `recjson` in `hstRECORD` history
table, however a similar dilemma remain: in case we introduce a new
metadata field, say "book price", do we want to reprocess all past
versions of all records to add this new field to archived versions?
Probably not.  However, if we decide to change deeply how to store
some field in JSON, we may want to adapt cached past versions too, so
that they would work with latest version of the client software.  This
is similar to how we planned to increase record API versioning: a
hypothetical example, if we change storage technique from:

```
<author>
  <name>Joe Doe</name>
</author>
```

to:

```
<author>
  <name>
    <firstname>Joe</firstname>
    <familyname>Doe</familyname>
  </name>
</author>
```

then the API version must change.  Similarly, if recjson configuration
changes dramatically, we may want to adapt past versions as well.

Using similar analogy, we have:

```
record_master_format + Record.create() + atlantis.cfg -> recjson
```

with two techniques: (a) we could version `atlantis.cfg` file and use
past record master formats to recreate past recjsons; (b) we could
store past versions of recjson as we go, like we do for marcxml and
hstRECORD.  The former is more economical on resources, the latter
better preserves history in case of important configuration changes.
(And captures any KB and SQL calls the format may be doing.)  This is
"best effort" vs "complete support" dilemma once again.

Considering recjson is internal master format, it is important to
provide flexibility.  Similarly, each site could decide based on a
configuration flag like `CFG_BIBUPLOAD_SERIALIZE_RECORD_STRUCTURE`.

Task 4: optionally archive also past recjson versions upon metadata
change and provide suitable API functions that would expose them (and
check their consistency against current field rules).

Speaking of recjson, additional challenge comes from calculated
fields, such as `number_of_citations`, `number_of_borrowers`.  It
would be useful to store these, but it must be very clear that these
are historical versions as of `YYYY..ss` timestamp.  This could become
actually another interesting use case, to be able to take a historical
snapshot of records at some wanted point in time, not only when a
record metadata changes, but also on demand, say each first day of
each month.  Kind of `bibedit --take-historical-snapshot` kind of API
that could be called from within bibupload, but also from time to time
to say keep track of the time dependence of the various calculated
fields such as number of citations or borrowers.

(This latter use case approaches the timely snapshotting of record
metadata and any interesting associated information for the Memento
RFC 7089 support, which will be addressed in other tickets.)

issue_title: WebSearch: 'e' method in search_unit()
issue_body:
Currently when one uses `search_unit(p=... f=..., m='e')` the current code (in legacy):
```python
    elif m == 'a' or m == 'r' or f == 'subject':
        # we are doing either phrase search or regexp search
    [...]
    else:
        # we are doing bibwords search by default
        hitset = search_unit_in_bibwords(p, f, wl=wl)
```

makes search_unit() to use `search_unit_in_bibwords()` (i.e. the WORD index), instead of `search_unit_in_idxphrases()` as intended.

Is this intended or a bug?

issue_title: Web "kickstart" script doesn't take hostname into account for nginx config.
issue_body:
While installing the Invenio Web host via the provided script, I've encountered an error due to nginx configuration.

The installed configuration file (`/etc/nginx/conf.d/invenio.conf`) didn't have the correct hostname inside for the `proxy_pass` line. In my case the directive was `proxy_pass http://web:5000;`, but my hostname was `invenio-web` and the server has failed to start. After correcting the hostname server started.

issue_title: tests: simplify doctest execution
issue_labels: [{'id': 414884581, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-base/labels/Size:%20easy', 'name': 'Size: easy', 'color': 'bc3cbc', 'default': False}, {'id': 414884500, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/inveniosoftware/invenio-base/labels/Status:%20ready%20for%20work', 'name': 'Status: ready for work', 'color': '149414', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
The following cookiecutter change:

   https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98

should be propagated to this Invenio module.

Namely, in `run-tests.sh`, the sphinx for doctests is invoked after pytest run:

```console
$ tail -3 ./\{\{\ cookiecutter.project_shortname\ \}\}/run-tests.sh
sphinx-build -qnNW docs docs/_build/html && python setup.py test && sphinx-build -qnNW -b doctest docs docs/_build/doctest
```

This sometimes led to problems on Travis CI with the second sphinx-build run due
to "disappearing" dependencies after the example application was tested.

A solution that worked for invenio-marc21 (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49#issuecomment-281287103)
and that was integrated in cookiecutter (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/cookiecutter-invenio-module/pull/98) was to
run doctest execution in pytest, removing the second `sphinx-build` invocation.

This both solved Travis CI build failures and simplified test suite execution.

Note that this change may necessitate to amend the code tests etc so that things
would be executed with the Flask application context (see
https://github.com/inveniosoftware/invenio-marc21/pull/49/commits/09e98fc18fd5591dc65d806286e8da51b7e384b4).

issue_title: Pure-Python ExtensionClass wraps __parent__ but C does not
issue_body:
Commit afb8488e966752dc3393124504db8eded128f99e made the C implementation of `ExtensionClass.Base` not wrap `__parent__` objects, but the pure python version still does. I ran into this while working on issue zopefoundation/Acquisition#3. (I'm working around it there for now; if I get time I'll submit a pull request here).

Compare the expected behaviour:
```
$ python

>>> import ExtensionClass
>>> class I(ExtensionClass.Base):
...     def __of__(self,o):
...         return 'wrapped'
...
>>> x = I()
>>> x.__parent__ = I()
>>> x.__parent__
<__main__.I object at 0x10f3b4290>
```

With the pure python behaviour:
```
$ PURE_PYTHON=1 python

>>> import ExtensionClass
>>> class I(ExtensionClass.Base):
...     def __of__(self, o):
...         return 'wrapped'
...
>>> x = I()
>>> x.__parent__ = I()
>>> x.__parent__
'wrapped'
```

issue_title: [revamp] Unpack archives that don't list their root directory
issue_body:
The code in `Recipe.unpack()` gets the first item on an archive's list as the root directory, and renames it if necessary. Archives like http://python.org/ftp/python/2.7.2/Python-2.7.2.tar.bz2 show their root directory as the first entry of `tar tjf`. But others like https://pypi.python.org/packages/source/T/Twisted/Twisted-15.4.0.tar.bz2 do not, and so instead the first file in the list gets renamed, causing the later build stage to fail (because the expected directory doesn't exist).

issue_title: python2 recipe always builds for armeabi regardless of what arch you tell it to target

issue_title: Python3 qtpy uses deprecated __version__ attribute from PySide
issue_body:
I am running openSUSE Tumbleweed and I have the following problem:
```pytb
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/qtpy/__init__.py", line 119, in <module>
    from PySide import __version__ as PYSIDE_VERSION  # analysis:ignore
ImportError: cannot import name '__version__'

During handling of the above exception, another exception occurred:

Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/usr/bin/spyder3", line 3, in <module>
    start.main()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/spyder/app/start.py", line 103, in main
    from spyder.app import mainwindow
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/spyder/app/mainwindow.py", line 49, in <module>
    requirements.check_qt()
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/spyder/requirements.py", line 39, in check_qt
    import qtpy
  File "/usr/lib/python3.6/site-packages/qtpy/__init__.py", line 125, in <module>
    raise PythonQtError('No Qt bindings could be found')
qtpy.PythonQtError: No Qt bindings could be found
```
It seems `qtpy` tries to import `__version__`, which was only available in the Python2 version of `PySide`. I had a quick `dir(PySide)` look at the available attributes and there isn't one that handles a version string.

Is it possible to prevent this and set the base IRI using some way so that the absolute IRI become the base IRI concatnate with the relative IRI ?

issue_title: generate correlated shear fields in redshift slices
issue_body:
The Gaussian random shear fields generated by GalSim according to some power spectrum implicitly assume some N(z) (determined by the user when they choose the input P(k)) and do not allow for the generation of a redshift-dependent shear field with proper correlation between redshift slices.

This paper:
http://adsabs.harvard.edu/abs/2004A%26A...417..873S
has a description of the algorithm for how to properly include multiple redshift slices and their correlations, which requires the user to know both the auto-power spectra and the cross-power spectra between their redshift slices.  See section 3.1 for details (they start talking about just a single Gaussian random field, but then talk about the generalization to multiple correlated ones).

I think that from a mathematical perspective this is pretty straightforward (just follow the recipe in the paper), and it's the design of the code and the user interface that is likely to be more complicated.  We've structured the Gaussian random shear field functionality around ```PowerSpectrum``` objects.  One option would be to have additional keyword arguments to specify not only the auto-power, but also a ```cross_power``` argument that takes a callable function, as well as a ```cross_field```` argument that takes the ```PowerSpectrum``` object (with cached shear field) for the other redshift slice.  However, this doesn't cleanly generalize to more than 2 redshift slices.  Another option would be to make a new kind of ```MultiPowerSpectrum``` object that can take some number of redshift slices, each with a ```PowerSpectrum``` and with cross-power-spectra also stored as ```PowerSpectrum``` objects (and used to make the shear fields for the redshift slices properly correlated).

Thoughts on whether this makes sense to include in GalSim, and how to go about it?

issue_title: galsim installation on Mac OSX 10.12
issue_body:
I recently (and perhaps foolishly) went to OSX 10.12 on my Mac, and am having some GalSim installation woes.  Note that I jumped directly from 10.9 because I had been worried about 10.10 when it came out, then I just kind of settled into a state of non-updating inertia for quite a while.  So if there are new issues for GalSim installation that came up at any point in 10.10, 10.11, or 10.12, they could all be relevant here. 

I ended up re-installing TMV 0.74 (previous was 0.72), boost 1.61 (previous was 1.49), and FFTW because the new OS seems to have caused all kinds of interesting things to happen on my system.

Initially I was seeing some issue with the GalSim installation that led me to follow the advice for boost that was given here: https://github.com/GalSim-developers/GalSim/wiki/Installation%20FAQ#what-should-i-do-about-undefined-symbols-for-architecture-x86_64-errors

When compiling, it was OK with the stuff in src/, though it did give me the same warning I mentioned on the TMV issues page:
```
clang: warning: libstdc++ is deprecated; move to libc++ with a minimum deployment target of OS X 10.9 [-Wdeprecated]
```

```
ld: warning: object file (/usr/local/lib/libfftw3.a(apiplan.o)) was built for newer OSX version (10.12) than being linked (10.5)
```
Does this mean it's linking to the wrong FFTW?  I looked up at the top and it said it found FFTW, but it didn't say where.   I don't understand why 10.5 would even be relevant, since I was on OSX 10.9 before this.

clang: warning: libstdc++ is deprecated; move to libc++ with a minimum deployment target of OS X 10.9 [-Wdeprecated]
Undefined symbols for architecture x86_64:
  "boost::python::objects::function_object(boost::python::objects::py_function const&, std::pair<boost::python::detail::keyword const*, boost::python::detail::keyword const*> const&)", referenced from:
      void boost::python::detail::def_init_aux<boost::python::class_<galsim::AngleUnit, boost::python::detail::not_specified, boost::python::detail::not_specified, boost::python::detail::not_specified>, boost::python::default_call_policies, boost::mpl::vector1<double>, boost::mpl::size<boost::mpl::vector1<double> > >(boost::python::class_<galsim::AngleUnit, boost::python::detail::not_specified, boost::python::detail::not_specified, boost::python::detail::not_specified>&, boost::mpl::vector1<double> const&, boost::mpl::size<boost::mpl::vector1<double> >, boost::python::default_call_policies const&, char const*, std::pair<boost::python::detail::keyword const*, boost::python::detail::keyword const*> const&) in Angle.os
```
and more like that, followed by
```
ld: symbol(s) not found for architecture x86_64
clang: error: linker command failed with exit code 1 (use -v to see invocation)
scons: *** [galsim/_galsim.so] Error 129
```

I did follow the instructions already on how to recompile boost.  (And I did the usual of explicitly telling it where my python lives, as well.)  Do you think this is definitely about boost or could it be related to the FFTW warning or ...? Thoughts?

issue_title: Name of cells is not saved in fst module
issue_body:
Here is an example. This problem has a significant impact on the analysis, since we cannot track back the cells that are analyzed.

```
from neurom import ezy
from neurom import fst

n = ezy.load_neuron('./test_data/neurolucida/sample.asc')
n1 = fst.load_neuron('./test_data/neurolucida/sample.asc')

n.name
Out: 'sample'

n1.name
AttributeError: 'Neuron' object has no attribute 'name'
```

The same applies for neurons in a population. We should be able to identify which neuron is analyzed, even when we study a whole population of them.

issue_title: CodePoint not working some icon
Icon request: push-pin
issue_body:
### What can it be used for?

A push-pin is used in user interfaces to indicate that a collapsible panel should stay open. The existing thumbtack icon does not meet the requirements because we need a pair, one icon to depict the unpushed state and another to depict the pushed-in state.

### Icon request checklist

- [x] This is a single icon or matched pair (Ex: `lock` / `unlock`)
- [x] The title starts with "Icon request: " and is followed by the requested icon name (Ex: `Icon request: magical-unicorn`)
- [x] I have [searched for existing issues](https://github.com/FortAwesome/Font-Awesome/issues) and to the best of my knowledge this is not a duplicate
- [ ] I have included at least one example image if it could be helpful (optional)
- [ ] The request is for a concrete object (it's harder to make an icon to represent happiness, it's easier to make a smiley face. ☺)

issue_title: Patroni can't start with python-consul > 0.7.0
issue_body:
This error is raised with I'm trying to start Patroni with python-consul 0.7.1:

```Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/opt/patroni/patroni.py", line 6, in <module>
    main()
  File "/opt/patroni/patroni/__init__.py", line 173, in main
    return patroni_main()
  File "/opt/patroni/patroni/__init__.py", line 140, in patroni_main
    patroni = Patroni()
  File "/opt/patroni/patroni/__init__.py", line 25, in __init__
    self.dcs = get_dcs(self.config)
  File "/opt/patroni/patroni/dcs/__init__.py", line 69, in get_dcs
    return item(config[name])
  File "/opt/patroni/patroni/dcs/consul.py", line 108, in __init__
    self._client = ConsulClient(host=host, port=port)
  File "/usr/lib/python2.7/site-packages/consul/base.py", line 305, in __init__
    self.http = self.connect(host, port, scheme, verify, cert)
TypeError: connect() takes at most 4 arguments (5 given)
```

In the older version of the library, code looked like this:
     self.http = self.connect(host, port, scheme, verify)

Current workaround for me is to freeze python-consul at 0.7.0.

issue_title: Make Deletion of recovery.conf Optional
issue_body:
When bootstrapping a cluster with the custom_replica creation, Patroni automatically deletes the  recovery.conf if it exists.  

When bootstrapping a replica from a pgBackRest restore, we would like to let pgBackRest generate the appropriate recovery.conf and have Patroni leave it in place.

To accomplish that, I'd like to add an optional parameter called `keep_existing_recovery_conf` (as below).  If that parameter is present and set to `True`, skip the deletion of the recovery.conf

```
bootstrap:
    method: <custom_bootstrap_method_name>
    <custom_bootstrap_method_name>:
        command: <path_to_custom_bootstrap_script> [param1 [, ...]]
        keep_existing_recovery_conf: True
```

issue_title: pyral 1.2.4 and 1.3.0: get() does not retrieve all work items
issue_body:
When calling rally.get() with a simple query string of a single custom field of type Yes/No/null, most of the matching work items are not retrieved. In all my get() tests, the custom field had a value of "Yes" as in 'myCustomField = "Yes"'. Identical results occurred whether I passed the query string using the paren syntax or as a list of a single string.

I tested with pyral 1.2.4 and 1.3.0. The requests package versions 2.12.5 and 2.13.0 were used and had no effect.

For example, for a particular Rally project, pyral 1.2.3 get() retrieves 43 work items. With pyral 1.2.4/1.3.0, get() retrieves 0 work items. The only change between tests was changing the pyral library.

Running the same tests but searching a different Rally project, pyral 1.2.3 retrieves 35 work items. The pyral 1.2.4/1.3.0 retrieves 1 work item.

Let me know if you require additional information.

I [tried that](https://circleci.com/gh/sgmap/mes-aides-ui/1725#artifacts/containers/0) and got [an error](https://1725-18399519-gh.circle-artifacts.com/0/tmp/circle-artifacts.w2gX7dm/stderr_npm_16211.txt) because `OpenFisca-Parsers` was not installed.

issue_title: Need help with Cognito user pool authentication + session cookie best practices
issue_body:
This is what I hacked together to be able to authenticate against an AWS Cognito user pool, and use the successful authentication to set a session cookie. It uses the built-in Cognito web UI for login: It works, but feels a lot clunkier than it should be .. for example maybe I should be using federated AWS IAM access (how would I control the session length in that case? What would the max session length be?). Another thing that felt clunky was getting back the authorization code and having to convert it to a JWT token (actually a dictionary of multiple JTW tokens) The JWT tokens weren't even valid, so I had to set `verify=False` when decoding. They also showed as the signatures not being verified on jwt.io, although it was a bit confusing because it showed an array of libraries, some of which seemed to be verified. I'm sharing this in case this helps anyone (saw the posts on AWS Cognito docs [needing work](https://github.com/aws/chalice/issues/563)), and would love to feedback on how to simplify this and follow best practices! If this isn't the best forum to post this, please let me know where to repost. ```python from chalice import Chalice from chalice import Response from chalice import CognitoUserPoolAuthorizer import Cookie import random import datetime import requests import jwt from jinja2 import Template app = Chalice(app_name='chalice-webapp') # AWS Cognito "client id" under AWS Cognito web admin / General Settings / App clients / App client id CLIENT_ID = "*********************" # This is the URL of the API after it's deployed to API Gateway. If you haven't yet deployed this to API Gateway # via "chalice deploy", then you can't set this. Resolve the catch22 by running "chalice deploy", grabbing the resulting # url, and then updating this variable. API_URL = "https://*******.execute-api.us-east-1.amazonaws.com/api" # The AWS Cognito domain prefix. For a given Cognito user pool, corresponds to General Settings / App Integration / App Domain COGNITO_DOMAIN_PREFIX = "mydomain" # The AWS region where you defined your Cognito user pool COGNITO_REGION = "us-east-1" # How long the session cookie should last COOKIE_EXPIRATION_DELTA = datetime.timedelta(days=1) # The Cognito URL for this domain. The Cognito hosted login endpoint is here, as well as the endpoint to convert the Authorization Code -> JWT Tokens COGNITO_DOMAIN_URL = "https://{}.auth.{}.amazoncognito.com".format(COGNITO_DOMAIN_PREFIX, COGNITO_REGION) @app.route('/') def index(): if not has_valid_session(app.current_request): return show_login_page() response_body = "

Welcome!

" response_body += "Here are the things you can do" return Response(body=response_body, headers={ "Content-Type": "text/html" }) def has_valid_session(req): # TODO: There should be a list of valid sessions stored somewhere and # TODO: the session ID should be checked against that. For now it just makes sure # TODO: there is a session cookie at all set by this domain. Not very secure. req = app.current_request if "cookie" in req.headers: cookie_str = req.headers["cookie"] if "session" in cookie_str: return True return False def show_login_page(): # Here is the login URL that is on the hosted Cognito UI # After login, it will redirect back to this API, to the /set-cookie endpoint, and pass a 'code' param, # which is the Authorization Code that can be exchanged for a JWT token login_url = "{}/login?redirect_uri={}/set-cookie&response_type=code&client_id={}".format( COGNITO_DOMAIN_URL, API_URL, CLIENT_ID ) login_page_template = """

You are not logged in. You must login to continue

Login """ template = Template(login_page_template) response_body = template.render(login_url=login_url) return Response(body=response_body, headers={ "Content-Type": "text/html" }) # The set-cookie page is called back after the Cognito login flow is completed. # It is the redirect_url of the Cognito App. # # In my particular case, I don't have any other authorization requirements other than # "the user was able to login over cognito". I have self-registration turned off, and # only a small number of users, who are all basically admin users. I just want to make # sure that the user has successfully completed the authentication song-and-dance at COGNITO_DOMAIN_URL, # and then give them a long lived session cookie. @app.route('/set-cookie') def set_cookie(): req = app.current_request # Get the Authorization code parameter that is passed by Cognito after a successful login code = "None" if "code" in req.query_params: code = req.query_params["code"] else: raise Exception("No authorization code parameter found in request. You must login first") # TODO: verify the code passed back from cognito auth flow print("/set-cookie context: {}. headers: {}".format(app.current_request.context, req.headers)) # Convert the Authorization Code -> JWT Tokens using the token # endpoint per these docs: https://docs.aws.amazon.com/cognito/latest/developerguide/token-endpoint.html payload = { 'grant_type': 'authorization_code', 'client_id': CLIENT_ID, 'redirect_uri': '{}/set-cookie'.format(API_URL), 'code': code } token_endpoint_url = "{}/oauth2/token".format(COGNITO_DOMAIN_URL) r = requests.post(token_endpoint_url, data=payload) # The response will look like this: # # { # "id_token": "hfNPReRmMP28g", # "access_token": "eyJraWQiOiw", # "refresh_token": "eyJjdHkiOiJKV1QiUbmg", # "expires_in": 3600, # "token_type": "Bearer" # } # # Get the id_token field id_token = r.json()["id_token"] # Decode the id_token # TODO: figure out why verify=False is required. Why can't signature be verified? decoded_id_token = jwt.decode(id_token, '', algorithms=['RS256'], verify=False) # Make sure there is a "cognito:username" field in the id_token if not "cognito:username" in decoded_id_token: raise Exception("Invalid id_token. Missing cognito:username field") if not "aud" in decoded_id_token: raise Exception("Invalid id_token. Missing cognito:username field") aud = decoded_id_token["aud"] if aud != CLIENT_ID: raise Exception("Invalid id_token. aud value does not match expected.") response_body = "

Session Created

" response_body += "Code: {}".format(code) return Response(body=response_body, headers={ "Set-Cookie": generate_cookie_header_val(), "Content-Type": "text/html" }) # Generate a cookie header value def generate_cookie_header_val(): cookie = Cookie.SimpleCookie() cookie["session"] = random.randint(0, 1000000000) expiration = datetime.datetime.now() + COOKIE_EXPIRATION_DELTA cookie["session"]["expires"] = expiration.strftime("%a, %d-%b-%Y %H:%M:%S PST") cookie["session"]["path"] = "/" # Workaround the fact that cookie.output returns a string like: 'Set-Cookie: session=650406237; etc..' # but we don't want the 'Set-Cookie: ' part in the actual header. raw_cookie_output = cookie.output() cookie_str = raw_cookie_output.replace("Set-Cookie: ", "") return cookie_str ```

issue_title: bug in BatchedExecutionEngine::backward: null ndEdfs for late parameter nodes
issue_body:
I'm getting null reference errors in BatchedExecutionEngine::backward, in the loop

for (VariableIndex i : cg.parameter_nodes)
    static_cast<ParameterNodeBase*>(cg.nodes[i])->accumulate_grad(ndEdfs[i]);

because ndEdfs[I]->v is null (this happens rarely, but consistently).

I believe this is the cause:

At the end of the BatchedExecutionEngine backward function, *all* of the parameter nodes' backward gradients are accumulated. But, the backward only computes the backward gradients for batches that are computed before whatever batch the "from_where" variable is a part of. 

My final loss is the last node added to the computation graph, but I think with batching it could end up in an earlier batch than some parameter nodes that were added in the same round (between calls to incremental_forward). I don't fully follow the batching code, so let me know if this sounds right. Perhaps this can only happen if those parameter nodes don't affect the final loss?

issue_title: Method for storing a volume in which the range gate differ between rays.
issue_body: Many radars collect volume such that the location of the range gates changes between rays most often between different sweeps.  For example some NEXRAD file change to a different gate spacing at high elevation.  Currently the CfRadial convention on which the Radar class is based has not manner for expressing a radar volume in which the gate locations vary by ray.  A method for expressing and storing such data should be developed and implemented in Py-ART.  This should be done in such a manner that the method can be included in or as an extension to the CfRadial format.

issue_title: Python 3.6 bug in gridding routines
issue_body:
Gridding routines appear to be broken in Python 3.6. In order to account for mobile radars, Py-ART Radar object allows arrays for radar lat/lon/alt, altho a lot of code heritage assumes scalars for these parameters. It appears that doing a float conversion on the z_disp calculation, or if that is removed, the appending immediately after this calculation raises a TypeError in 3.6 due to this array/scalar confusion. Here is the error below. This completely breaks gridding.

----> 2                 xlim=(-100000, 50000), ylim=(0, 150000), grid_shape=(20, 151, 151))
      3 g2 = grid_radar(r2, origin=(r2.latitude['data'][0], r2.longitude['data'][0]),
      4                 xlim=(-100000, 50000), ylim=(0, 150000), grid_shape=(20, 151, 151))

---> 13         gridding_algo='map_gates_to_grid', grid_origin_alt=0.0)

issue_title: Exception thrown when Useragent is longer than 255
Store the reason the user has been deactivated
issue_body:
There are several reason we can deactivate the user for:

  * user account expired
  * user password expired
  * user entered incorrect password consecutively too many times

Currently, we just log the reason using python logging and deactivate the user by setting `is_active` to `True`.

It would be an improvement to save the reason why the user has been deactivated in the database, so it can be shown in admin and allowing the administrators to find out what happened without having to dig into logs.

issue_title: [makeotf] GDEF should not contain GlyphClassDef entries with class 0
Widget Rule definitions should allow day, day of month, month selection for all frequencies
issue_body:
### tl;dr
The dateutil docs give examples like:
```
list(rrule(YEARLY, interval=4, count=3, bymonth=11,
               byweekday=TU, bymonthday=(2,3,4,5,6,7,8),
               dtstart=parse("19961105T090000")))
[datetime.datetime(1996, 11, 5, 9, 0),
 datetime.datetime(2000, 11, 7, 9, 0),
 datetime.datetime(2004, 11, 2, 9, 0)]
```
but we can't specify months AND days of week in the same rule in the django-recurrence widget

### Detail
I need to specify a range for certain days of the week, but only for particular months of the year (for seasonal activities). I can achieve an example of this in python with:

    list(rrule(YEARLY, bymonth=(1,2,3,7,9), byweekday=(SA,SU), count=10))

(list and count included for readability of output)

As awesome as django-recurrence is, each frequency tab (eg Annually, Weekly) only supports options relative to that type, so Annually doesn't let me specify for which weekdays, for example. I can add another rule, but that is a logical OR which means that dates are included if they match the first rule or the second rule.

One way around this would be for me to use an Exclude, but this seems to only be a single day in the month (when I choose Annually and my months). Let me know if I'm missing something here.

Another way, which would be an enhancement to the widget. It seems from the dateutil docs that the Frequency selected is only relevant for *interval* and *bysetpos*. This means that any Frequency can have day of week, week of year, etc. So the widget would need reworking so that regardless of Annually / Monthly / Weekly / Daily selected, the month, day of month, and day of week selectors would always be visible.

Discussion welcome

issue_title: PubSub: Pubsub Python Client stops acking messages after several seconds
issue_body:
# Thank you for reporting an issue to google-cloud-python!

If you are reporting an issue or requesting a feature, please search the
existing open and closed issues to see if there is already work being done.

- https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/issues
- http://stackoverflow.com/questions/tagged/google-cloud-python

If you can provide us with as much of the following information as possible it
will help us identify the cause of your issue more quickly.

1. Specify the API at the beginning of the title (for example, "BigQuery: ...")
   General, Core, and Other are also allowed as types
2. OS type and version
python:3-alpine docker image running in GKE
3. Python version and virtual environment information `python --version`
4. google-cloud-python version `pip show google-cloud`, `pip show google-<service>` or `pip freeze`
google-cloud-pubsub==0.28.4 
grpcio==1.6.3

5. Stacktrace if available
6. Steps to reproduce
Run example code, watch stackdriver metrics for acks vs modify_ack_deadline
7. Code example
```python
import os
import sys
import logging
import traceback
import json
import time
import argparse
import grequests
import grpc

from item_store import ItemStore

from google.cloud.pubsub_v1.subscriber.policy.thread import Policy
from google.cloud import pubsub_v1
requests = ItemStore()

class OurPolicy(Policy):
    """
    We occasionally see errors that google code doesn't
    recover from, set a flag that let's the outer thread respond
    by restarting the client.
    grpc._channel._Rendezvous: <_Rendezvous of RPC that terminated
    with (StatusCode.UNAVAILABLE, OS Error)>
    """
    _exception_caught = None
    def __init__(self, *args, **kws):
        return super(OurPolicy, self).__init__(*args, **kws)
    def on_exception(self, exc):
        # If this is DEADLINE_EXCEEDED, then we want to retry by returning
        # None instead of raise-ing
        deadline_exceeded = grpc.StatusCode.DEADLINE_EXCEEDED
        code_value = getattr(exc, 'code', lambda: None)()
        if code_value == deadline_exceeded:
            return
        OurPolicy._exception_caught = exc
        # will just raise exc
        return super(OurPolicy, self).on_exception(exc)

class InvalidSchemaException(Exception):
    pass

def log_unhandled_exception(type, value, traceback):
    logger.error(type, value, traceback)

sys.excepthook = log_unhandled_exception

def send_to_data_insertion(message):
    requests.add(grequests.post('http://%s:8080/url_here=%s' % (
        address, token),
        data=message.data.decode('utf-8')))


###############  Subscriber logic here ####################
def receive_messages(project, subscription_name):
    subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient(policy_class=OurPolicy)
    subscription_path = subscriber.subscription_path(
        project, subscription_name)

    def callback(message):
        message.ack()
        try:
            send_to_data_insertion(message)

        except InvalidSchemaException as e:
            return
        except Exception as e:
            return

    while live_forever:
        if subscriber is None:
            logger.warning('Starting pubsub subscriber client')
            subscriber = pubsub_v1.SubscriberClient(policy_class=OurPolicy)

        subscriber.subscribe(subscription_path).open(callback=callback)

        try:
            while True:
                grequests.map(requests.getAll())
                time.sleep(sleep_interval)

                if OurPolicy._exception_caught:
                    exc = OurPolicy._exception_caught
                    OurPolicy._exception_caught = None
                    raise exc
        except KeyboardInterrupt:
            break
        except Exception as e:
            subscriber = None

    # otherwise, sleep for one interval and exit
    time.sleep(sleep_interval)

#####################################################


if __name__ == "__main__":
    parser.add_argument('project', help='Google cloud project ID')
    parser.add_argument('subscription', help="Google cloud subscription name")
    args = parser.parse_args()
    receive_messages(args.project, args.subscription)
```

issue_title: chrome通过socks5协议与ss交互问题
issue_body:
亲爱的ss作者，你们好！
最近我正在写一篇关于chrome与shadowsocks数据交互的技术文章，遇到了以下问题：
1.打开一个chrome tab（在后续操作流程中我会一直在这个chrome tab上），输入www.google.com，直到google页面显示出来，在这期间，sslocal是否只帮我们创建了一个TCPRelay实例和多个TCPRelayHandler实例？
2.接着我用google搜索，直到所有搜索结果出来，在这期间所发的所有请求是否共用之前创建出来的TCPRelay实例来创建TCPRelayHandler实例？

**我自己的理解**是：chrome的每一个tab会对应一个TCPRelay，如果这个tab长时间不浏览网页，那么shadowsocks会自动销毁TCPRelay，当这个tab再次浏览网页时shadowosocks会重新创建一个TCPRelay；如果这个tab一直浏览网页，那么每次请求都会共用同一个TCPRelay，而每一个请求就对应一个TCPRelayHandler。
以上是我的困惑与理解，希望你们能帮忙给出解答帮助我完成这篇技术文章的解析。

When creating an RDS instance using a Cloudformation template, the "Engine" property should not be required when "DBSnapshotIdentifier" is set, according to the Cloudformation documentation here:
http://docs.aws.amazon.com/AWSCloudFormation/latest/UserGuide/aws-properties-rds-database-instance.html#cfn-rds-dbinstance-engine

However even when i have set "DBSnapshotIdentifier" i am getting the following error:
`ValueError: Resource Engine required in type AWS::RDS::DBInstance`
Which goes away when i set the "Engine" property.

Here is a minimal template demonstrating the issue:

```
#!/usr/bin/env python

from troposphere import Template
from troposphere.rds import DBInstance

template = Template()

rds_instance = template.add_resource(DBInstance(
    'MyDB',
    DBName='test',
    AllocatedStorage=25,
    DBInstanceClass='db.m4.large',
    DBSubnetGroupName='default',
    DBSnapshotIdentifier='id'
))

print template.to_json()
```

Thanks.

issue_title: Pex seems to mess with verbosity flag in Ansible
issue_body:
I'm not sure if this is even the right repository to file this issue, but I'm having an issue with `pex` and `ansible`, and I can't figure out what it is.

Basically, `ansible-playbook` works totally normally in every respect when I run it as a PEX binary, except that the verbosity flags are not respected at all.

We've dug into this with ipdb and the option seems to be set properly in Ansible everywhere we check, but something happens when it forks a task runner that causes it to not pass the option properly.

I've got it down to a pretty minimal reproduction case using Docker. Just copy this into a file and do

```bash
docker build -t pextest .
```

to confirm things not working as expected.

```dockerfile
# Dockerfile
FROM buildpack-deps:stretch

RUN apt-get update \
    && apt-get install -y \
    python-pip \
    python-dev

RUN pip install pex

RUN pex ansible -o ansible-playbook -c ansible-playbook

RUN pip install ansible

RUN echo "---\n- hosts: all\n  tasks:\n    - shell: echo hi" > /playbook.yml
RUN echo "[default]\nlocalhost ansible_connection=local" > /inventory

RUN echo "Working verbosity"
RUN ansible-playbook -vvv -i inventory playbook.yml

RUN echo "Verbosity not working"
RUN ./ansible-playbook -vvv -i inventory playbook.yml
```

Ideally `pex` wouldn't introduce any discrepancies in runtime behavior, which is why I bring this here. Perhaps we are building our executable wrong?

issue_title: AccessDenied: NoSuchEntityException when attaching AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole for a new Function
issue_body:
Hi Team,

I've just got a strange AccessDenied exception when creating a new `AWS::Serverless::Function`. I'm trying to add a function that picks up item updates from a DynamoDB stream and indexes them in CloudSearch. However, when updating the stack with that new function, CloudFormation fails with an 'AccessDenied' error, saying that
```
API: iam:AttachRolePolicy User: arn:aws:sts::************:assumed-role/infrastructure-BackendDeploymentRole-XXXXX/AWSCloudFormation is not authorized to perform: iam:AttachRolePolicy on resource: role backend-beta-CloudSearchUploadDocumentsRole-YYYYY
```
That is strange, because I have added permissions for `infrastructure-BackendDeploymentRole-XXXXX` to attach `AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole` to any of the roles with `backend-beta-` prefix, which are created automatically for the the corresponding functions by SAM, and this has worked for all other functions before.

Further, the CloudTrail log displays that this `AccessDenied` error is in fact
```
Error code: NoSuchEntityException
Resources Referenced (2):
IAM Role	backend-beta-CloudSearchUploadDocumentsRole-YYYYY	
IAM Policy	arn:aws:iam::aws:policy/service-role/AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole
```
So it appears that CloudFormation doesn't 'see' the role it has just created for this function when trying to attach `AWSLambdaBasicExecutionRole` to it. I don't know why and how such a race condition could occur, given that all functions before this one worked fine.

Here's my reference config, just in case:
```
  CloudSearchUploadDocuments:
    Type: AWS::Serverless::Function
    Properties:
      FunctionName: !Sub ${AWS::StackName}-CloudSearchUploadDocuments
      CodeUri: ../app
      Handler: search.uploadDocuments
      Runtime: nodejs6.10
      Environment:
        Variables:
          SEARCH_DOC_ENDPOINT: !GetAtt SetupSearchDomain.DocEndpoint
      Events:
        DatasetsTable:
          Type: DynamoDB
          Properties:
            Stream: !GetAtt DatasetsTable.StreamArn
            StartingPosition: TRIM_HORIZON
            BatchSize: 100
      Policies:
        Statement:
          - Effect: Allow
            Action:
              - dynamodb:ListStreams
              - dynamodb:DescribeStream
              - dynamodb:GetShardIterator
              - dynamodb:GetRecords
            Resource:
              - !Sub arn:aws:dynamodb:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:table/${DatasetsTable}/stream/*
          - Effect: Allow
            Action:
              - cloudsearch:UploadDocuments
            Resource:
              - !Sub arn:aws:cloudsearch:${AWS::Region}:${AWS::AccountId}:domain/${SearchDomain}
```

Thanks

issue_title: Problems with Russian letter Ё
issue_body:
As http://snowballstem.org/algorithms/russian/stemmer.html properly mentions, Russian alphabet contains letter Ё [jo] which is quite often replaced with Е, especially in regular, non-academic texts.

So indeed the beast approach is to replace Ё -> Е when stemming.

Now if you check the existing demo http://snowballstem.org/demo.html you can see that it doesn't actually happen.

Let's take Russian word for "honey" — «мёд», and its form with different ending — «мёдом».
If you paste it along with its "normalized" form (with Е) you can see that the form with Ё is not properly stemmed:
![demo](https://monosnap.com/file/7POF56REeDsTEQP9ZkLyYFOijBDgX9.png)

Here's sample input so that you can run the tests yourself: "мёд мёдом мед медом".

This is a serious problem when searching through the corpus of natural texts. Even if you're purist (like me in this case) and type all your search terms with properly placed Ё you won't be able to match the original texts that are using Е.

issue_title: imageio.reader maintains connection to file even after it's closed
issue_body:
So my use case is mounting a remote folder, which contains several video files, with sshfs. Then making a connection to the video files with imageio.reader. When I close my program I want to close the video files and unmount the remote folder. However imageio.reader seems to be maintaining a connection to the file even after it's closed. Here is a minimal code example

```
import subprocess
import imageio
import os

mount_command = 'sshfs -o ro -o allow_other -o IdentityFile={} {}@{}:{} {}'.format(
		key_file, host_username, host_ip, host_data_directory, local_data_directory)
subprocess.call(mount_command, shell=True)
video_file = os.path.join(local_data_directory, file_name)

This gives the error

issue_title: How to get a refresh_token?
issue_body:
Hi,

I'd like to get the `refresh_token` for the first time only so the app doesn't require subsequent authentication prompts (as this will be a script running in the background). 

It works the first time but after the token expires it gives an error:

```
 gauth.Refresh()
  File "/Library/Python/2.7/site-packages/pydrive/auth.py", line 369, in Refresh
    raise RefreshError('No refresh_token found.'
pydrive.auth.RefreshError: No refresh_token found.Please set access_type of OAuth to offline.
```

This is the code handling the authentication:
```python
# Sort out the authentication...
gauth = GoogleAuth()

# Try to load saved client credentials
gauth.LoadCredentialsFile(os.path.dirname(os.path.realpath(__file__)) + os.sep + "credentials.json")

if gauth.credentials is None:
    # Authenticate if they're not there
    gauth.LocalWebserverAuth()
elif gauth.access_token_expired:
    # Refresh them if expired
    gauth.Refresh()
else:
    # Initialize the saved creds
    gauth.Authorize()
```

Then I have a `settings.yaml`:

```yaml
client_config_backend: settings
client_config:
  client_id: xxx
  client_secret: xxx

save_credentials: True
save_credentials_backend: file
save_credentials_file: <path_to_save_credentials.json>

get_refresh_token: True

oauth_scope:
  - https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.file
  - https://www.googleapis.com/auth/drive.install
```

I believe `client_secrets.json` is not required if you have `settings.yaml`, is that correct?

Any ideas why `refresh_token` is always `NULL` in the response `credentials.json`?

$ ipwb replay mkdc.cdxj
Executing first-run procedure on provided sample data.
 * Running on http://127.0.0.1:5000/ (Press CTRL+C to quit)
Retrieving URI-Ms from mkdc.cdxj
File mkdc.cdxj does not exist locally, fetching remote
No scheme in path, assuming IPFS hash and fetching...

```

IPWB replay is treating the filename as an IPFS hash.

This was likely introduced when specifying an IPFS hash was implemented. All scenarios require more automatic testing.

issue_title: Update tests to use the latest version of ipfs, 0.4.12
Shutdown the ipfs daemon more gracefully
issue_body:
When stopping the ipfs daemon, ipwb currently (https://github.com/oduwsdl/ipwb/blob/master/ipwb/replay.py#L107) runs a `killall ipfs` command via Python subprocess. go-ipfs introduced an `ipfs shutdown` command in version 0.4.10 per https://ipfs.io/blog/27-go-ipfs-0-4-10/

Leverage this if the version of the daemon ≥ 0.4.10. Checking the daemon version will be critical here, as the killall command may still needing in go-ipfs versions < version 0.4.10. This can be accomplished by performing `ipfs shutdown` in a try then forcibly perform the killall if it fails.

issue_title: TargetingCampaignId not working for Price Extension update
Add the ability to host content on arbitrary URLs
issue_body: Many services such as Google Webmaster Tools require you to add specific content at a website endpoint to prove domain ownership. This typically requires updating the root URLconf and adding a template file to the project, followed by an app update. It would be much more useful if the CMS had a module, similar to the redirects module, which took an arbitrary URL and returned specified content (could just be text/plain content).

issue_title: Add option for secondary layer index
issue_body: The `charm-build` command already accepts an `--interface-service` option to change where layers are looked up, but if a charm author wants to leverage that to use a set of private layers or layers for testing, they have to mirror the entire index.  It would be good if the option was split into `--index-url` and `--index-overrides-url`, where layers would be looked up in the overrides index first if that option is provided and only in the main index if not found in the overrides index.

issue_title: Specify the shapes to the analysis step
issue_body:
If the user has extra information about the shapes of the tensors, there is no need to run deep analysis.

The analyze method will be modified with an extra argument `shapes=<dict>` with `dict` a mapping from column names (strings) to lists, each list corresponding to a cell shape. The limitation of this approach is that it will not infer the sizes of the blocks if they are the same, but this is not a hindrance in practice.

Possible optimization: if there is only one numerical column in the dataframe, the user can directly pass the list of dimension instead of a dictionary.

issue_title: Add default parameter metadata to NI-DMM
issue_body:
Go through the API with a domain expert, and decide for each function which parameters should have default values in the Python API and what those values should be.

This needs to be done carefully, because in Python, all parameters with default values must go at the end of the parameter list. So changing this later would likely be a source breaker for client code.

This will most likely be a subset of Recommended/Optional input terminals on corresponding LabVIEW VIs.

issue_title: Restore button on django-admin doesn't work
Music21Object.id problem
issue_body:
The id of all objects is settable, and this is great.  However, there are 538 places in the code where the id(object)  is used (object could be anything such as c, a, ...)  so this circumvents using the assigned ID.  

My initial discovery is due the fact that I assigned IDs when building up a score using loops and function calls, then in stream/core.py _addElementPreProcess id(element) is tested instead of element.id when writing to MusicXML or MIDI.

Python object ids are not guaranteed to be unique  during the life of the program so they should not be persistent across function calls.  

I suggest this initial fix:

in music21/base.py:
    `
     from uuid import uuid4
`
then at line 396 (or so):

```
        if "id" in keywords:
            self.id = keywords["id"]
        else:
            self.id = 'm21-'+str(uuid4())

```
This produces a unique id that can be persisted and used as needed. 

In stream/core.py line 280, replace

            idElement = id(element)
with
            idElement = element.id

also in the exception change id(element) to element.id 

I am sure that there are other issues like this lurking because of the id(object) checks.  But I haven't seen them yet. 


Thanks.

issue_title: Allow running code after spew() iterates, but before it signals we're done
issue_body:
Consider a pipeline that ends with 2 processors:

* ...
* dump.to_zip
* upload_zip

`dump.to_zip` writes a datapackage as a zip in the local filesystem, which `upload_zip` loads and uploads to some service. This means that they have a dependency: `upload_zip` can't run before `dump.to_zip` is finished.

The design of datapackage-pipelines is to work with streaming data. Every processor runs in parallel, taking the data rows as they come. The current pattern that we use to make sure the previous processors have finished running is to call `spew()` to pass the data on to the next processors (modified or not), and then run our code. This works fine if there's only one processor that uses this pattern, but not if there's more than one. Consider this simplified code for `dump.to_zip` and `upload_zip`.

* `dump.to_zip`
```python
zip_file = zipfile.ZipFile(out_file)

spew(
    datapackage,
    resource_iter  # This iterator will write the files to `zip_file`
)

# After spew() has finished, it'll unblock the following processor's spew() calls
# so the code after their spew() calls will start running.

zip_file.close()
```

* `upload_zip`
```python
# Block until the previous processor's spew() calls have finished
spew(datapackage, resource_iter)

# Load zip from path and upload it
upload_zip(params['in_file'])
```

This doesn't guarantee that `dump.to_zip` will call `zip_file.close()` before `upload_zip` read the zip file, so it might be reading an invalid zip from disk (because `dump.to_zip` is still writing the contents). We have a race condition.

This race condition happens because the processors are communicating outside `datapackage-pipelines`, using the filesystem. As `dpp` doesn't know about this, it has no way to coordinate their communication. To avoid this, we must get the code in `dpp`.

My proposal is to add a `finalizer` parameter to `spew()` where you can send finalization code to be executed after `spew()` has done processing the incoming data, but before it has signaled to the next processors in the pipeline that we're finished. This way, the code for (for example) `dump.to_zip` would become:

```python
zip_file = zipfile.ZipFile(out_file)

spew(
    datapackage,
    resource_iter,
    finalizer=lambda: zip_file.close()
)
```

I have implemented this pattern on https://github.com/openspending/datapackage-pipelines-fiscal/commit/391048159ad21e647e7e5b4b316467848e3d2bf3, although as that's outside `dpp`, I had to do it in a hackish way.

issue_title: Small bug in writeLP, easy to fix -- Andres Sommerhoff
issue_body:
Hi Stu, just found a small bug in writeLP() of file pulp.py.

In line 1501 dummyVar is used to remember that a dummy variable was necessary to fix the objective affine expression. If there was no need to fix the objective then dummyVar is set to None. Later on, in line 1570 the use of the dummyVar is reverted (by subtracting dummyVar from objective if dummyVar was not None).  The problem is that the line 1512 uses the same "dummyVar" name but for solving a constrain issue. This means that Objective's dummyVar will be overwritten when constrains need to be fixed, making impossible to recover previous objective in line 1570 (actually the objective will end with a new dummy LPvariable included on it). 

The fix is easy. For example, just to rename "dummyVar" to "ObjectiveDummyVar" in line 1501 and line 1570. I will appreciate you can fix this, to avoid some workaround I have done today.

Best regards, Andres

1501         wasNone, dummyVar = self.fixObjective()   
1502         objName = self.objective.name 
1503         if not objName: objName = "OBJ" 
1504         f.write(self.objective.asCplexLpAffineExpression(objName, constant = 0)) 
1505         f.write("Subject To\n") 
1506         ks = list(self.constraints.keys()) 
1507         ks.sort() 
1508         for k in ks: 
1509             constraint = self.constraints[k] 
1510             if not list(constraint.keys()): 
1511                 #empty constraint add the dummyVar 
1512                 dummyVar = self.get_dummyVar() 
1513                 constraint += dummyVar 
1514                 #set this dummyvar to zero so infeasible problems are not made feasible 
1515                 f.write((dummyVar == 0.0).asCplexLpConstraint("_dummy"))
.....
1568         f.write("End\n")

1570         self.restoreObjective(wasNone, dummyVar)

issue_title: Proposal: Plan for improving testing of Deisctl
issue_body:
#### Main

- Focuses on STDOUT rather than what the parsing functions return. I think that testing functions, such as parseArgs, removeGlobalArgs, and isGobalArg directly as opposed to testing output is a better strategy because it shows what function is failing, not that there are problems somewhere in the code that cause the output to differ from what you expect.

- This would also make the tests less brittle, currently tests like TestHelp have copied and pasted expected output from other parts of the code.  

#### Backend

- There is a partial fleet stub already done. We should finish the stub and add tests for all fleet operations.

#### Client

- I think we should move the command line parsing from CMD to client, and then call CMD with the parsed values. This will make it easier for us to test the CMD package and separate concerns.

#### CMD

- Currently there is a partial stub of the backend. We should finish this stub and add tests for all the deisctl operations.

#### Config

- ETCD should probably be broken out into its own sub-package. ETCD could be useful out of the config package, for example, for a future `deisctl backup`.

- Config has almost no tests. The etcd client could be stubbed or mocked, allowing us to properly test the rest of the config methods.

#### Thoughts

- We have thrown around the idea of moving away from docopt. Whether or not we decide to do that, most of my suggestions should still hold up, we will still need to put more work into mocking and stubbing the backend.

- Mocking vs Stubbing: Currently all the deisctl tests use stubbing on things like fleet and the backend. I think that mocks might be less work than reimplementing the entire backend. I'm not very experienced with go testing, so you guys would know better than I what the best approach would be. 

- This is just stuff I saw and is primarily my own opinion. I'd really like advice on whether these actions make sense or if I missed a area to improve testing.

issue_title: Proposal: Make Deis compatible with large-scale, production cluster architecture
issue_body:
Apologies... this is long and detailed, but (I think) comprehensive...

Because building etcd consensus can be time-consuming when the number of nodes in an etcd cluster is large, CoreOS recommends the following architecture for production and/or large scale deployments:

https://coreos.com/docs/cluster-management/setup/cluster-architectures/#production-cluster-with-central-services

In such an architecture, only a small, fixed number of nodes run etcd while all other nodes (an elastic pool of "workers") are merely configured with a list of etcd peers they can speak to.

Unfortunately, Deis doesn't play nicely with this cluster architecture fresh out of the box because Deis components generally expect that etcd is running locally.  Some components allow you to configure your way around this on some level, but generally do not go far enough in that regard.

Ultimately, I have overcome these challenges, but it required quite a significant investment in customizing images and unit files.  That's not tenable in the long-term for operators who wish to upgrade Deis frequently and don't want to have to re-apply their customizations with each release.

Here, I want to enumerate the few issues that comprise this (each on its own is pretty minor) and then propose a simple solution, which I would be happy to work on.

The following components are those that would run on worker nodes without etcd running locally:

* `deis-logspout`
* `deis-publisher`
* Arguably, `deis-router` (since it may need to scale significantly)
* Applications

Issues arising on such worker nodes:

1. `deis-logspout` and `deis-publisher`'s unit files require `etcd.service`.  This imposes a constraint that etcd be running locally.

2.  `get_image` script (and raw `etcdctl`) don't work from within unit files-- this can interfere with resolving custom image names for `deis-logspout`, `deis-publisher`, and `deis-router` (if applicable).  In the case of applications (with unit files created by `deis-controller`), image names cannot be resolved at all.

3. Most components (their containers) can be passed an `ETCD_HOST` environment variable that will allow them to speak to a remote etcd.  This is good, but does not go far enough.  It creates a single point of failure on that one host and also means unit files need to be modified to assign a specific IP address to the `ETCD_HOST` environment variable.  That sort of customization-per-cluster is something we should want to avoid.

4. `MachineMetadata` that articulates a Deis component's assignment to a "common services" node vs. a "worker" node isn't present in existing component unit files.  For an application, it's present only if explicitly added via `deis tags:set`.

Here are simple solutions to each of these, in turn:

1. `deis-logsout`, `deis-publisher`'s unit files can be modified to "want" `etcd.service` instead of requiring it.  etcd's absence from a system will no longer be an issue, but on systems where `etcd` _is_ scheduled, it will still come up first.  (`After` clause would not be touched.)

2. The `ETCDCTL_PEERS` environment variable can be used anywhere `etcdctl` is installed to tell etcdctl to use that list of peers instead of assuming etcd is running locally.  I've seen this work brilliantly on worker nodes.  I inject this into the environment using `cloud-config` and a custom `/etc/profile.d/etcdctl.sh`.  The trouble is that this only helps a logged in user.  The value isn't visible to systemd.  That can be changed, however, by using the same strategy (`cloud-config`) to _optionally_ inject `ETCDCTL_PEERS` into the `/etc/environment` file where Deis components' unit files already source environment variables from today.  The unit files for applications would need to be modified to include `EnvironmentFile=/etc/environment` as well-- they do not currently.  Once this is set, `etcdctl` and the Deis `get_image` script will begin working within unit files on machines where etcd is not running.

3. As stated before, it is `deis-logspout`, `deis-publisher`, and `deis-router` that are most affected by this.  With `ETCDCTL_PEERS` environment variable (potentially) set via no. 2 above, this environment variable can also be passed to each of those components using `-e ETCD_PEERS=$ETCDCTL_PEERS`.  Only slight modifications would be required to the Go code for each of those components to make them prefer the list of etcd peers mentioned by `ETCD_PEERS` (if set) over the singular etcd host that might otherwise be mentioned by `ETCD_HOST`.

4. fleet assigns each unit to nodes that match _any_ (not all) of the `MachineMetadata` constraints.  As such, we could use the following for any unit that must run _only_ on "common services nodes":

```
[X-Fleet]
MachineMetadata="deisRole=general"
MachineMetadata="deisRole=control"
```

Or this for any unit that must run _only_ on worker nodes:

```
[X-Fleet]
MachineMetadata="deisRole=general"
MachineMetadata="deisRole=worker"
```

Existing example user-data (`cloud-config`) could be updated to assign a `deisRole` of `general` to all nodes.

With all these changes in play, the fresh-out-of-the-box experience for a novice operator on a  small cluster is not complicated or tarnished in any way.  For a more skilled operator wanting to run Deis on a larger, production cluster, `cloud-config` becomes a one-stop shop for partitioning their cluster into "common services" and "worker" nodes-- which is not a stretch because I believe most who are running (or want to run Deis) at any sort of scale are already doing custom things with their user-data /  `cloud-config`.

Does anyone have any thoughts on this?  I think it's a relatively simple and comprehensive approach.  If there are any gaping holes in it, I am eager to be called out on it!  :)

issue_title: Set a minimum polling interval on `transfer task wait`
issue_body:
Allowing sub-second polling may be desirable -- a strong case can be made for writing a script which does
```shell
# start transfer
transfer_out="$(globus transfer async-transfer --format JSON ...)"
# parse & extract task ID
task_id="$(echo "$transfer_out" | jq ...)"
# wait for task to complete
echo "waiting on task $task_id"
globus transfer task wait --task-id "$task_id" --polling-interval 0.1 --heartbeat
# do more stuff...
...
```

I don't think that forcing someone running this kind of script to wait up to 1 second for a task to complete is entirely fair. If they're running many small tasks in succession, that cost will escalate to significant delays.

However, there is really no case to justify
```shell
globus transfer task wait --task-id "$task_id" --polling-interval 0.001 --heartbeat
```

If you make a call like this, you've grossly misunderstood the performance characteristics of the CLI and are unnecessarily hammering the API.


I think that making `0.1` the minimum allowed would be reasonable -- it means that going into hundredths-digit precision is a clear-cut sign that you're probably doing it wrong without being too limiting.
